title: GPDE Workshop - Positive mass theorem for asymptotically hyperbolic manifolds with some corners
speaker: Jie Qing

proof_stages.label: corner framework and asymptotically flat prototype
proof_stages.summary: The talk defines a Riemannian manifold with a corner along a hypersurface, recalls the asymptotically flat positive mass theorem and Miao's corner version, and motivates the mean-curvature jump condition through the Riccati/scalar-curvature formula.

proof_stages.label: asymptotically hyperbolic mass and theorem statement
proof_stages.summary: The asymptotically hyperbolic expansion, mass inequality, spin or low-dimensional hypotheses, scalar-curvature threshold R >= -n(n-1), and the corner positive mass theorem to be proved are stated.

proof_stages.label: three-step proof reduction
proof_stages.summary: The proof is reduced to smoothing the corner, conformally repairing any scalar-curvature loss, and applying the smooth asymptotically hyperbolic positive mass theorem while tracking the mass aspect.

proof_stages.label: smoothing the corner with controlled scalar curvature
proof_stages.summary: A smoothed metric g_epsilon is constructed that agrees with the original metric away from a small tubular neighborhood of the hypersurface, converges in C^0, preserves the end, and has negative scalar-curvature part uniformly bounded and supported near the corner.

proof_stages.label: linear conformal repair of the scalar curvature
proof_stages.summary: Instead of solving a nonlinear Yamabe-type equation, the proof solves a linear equation whose potential is the negative scalar-curvature defect, uses weighted Hölder Fredholm theory and small L^{n/2} norm to get a positive solution, and thereby conformally raises scalar curvature to at least -n(n-1).

proof_stages.label: mass-aspect change under the conformal deformation
proof_stages.summary: A calculation with the geodesic defining function shows that if the conformal factor has leading term a(theta) rho^n, then the transformed mass-aspect tensor differs from the original one by an explicit multiple of a(theta)g_0, reducing the theorem to controlling this coefficient.

proof_stages.label: hyperbolic parametrix and transplantation to the end
proof_stages.summary: The proof constructs a PDE parametrix by writing the fundamental solution of the model hyperbolic operator, transplanting it to the asymptotically hyperbolic manifold with coordinates chosen to match the volume delta, and estimating the resulting error kernel both near the pole and at infinity.

proof_stages.label: representation formula and smallness of the leading coefficient
proof_stages.summary: A Green representation formula, asymptotic limits of the hyperbolic kernel, angular decompositions near infinity, and decay estimates for the source terms show that the coefficient a(theta) is small as epsilon tends to zero, so the conformally corrected mass tends to the original mass.

examples.name: hemisphere glued to the exterior of a disk
examples.note: A hemisphere is glued along its equator to the complement of the unit disk to make the notion of a metric corner along a hypersurface concrete.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker uses the hemisphere replacing a disk as a picture of a corner and identifies the circle/exterior pieces, but does not compute or verify anything on it

passes.reason: A round hemisphere glued along a round circle to a Euclidean exterior is a highly regular rotationally symmetric model of a corner, used illustratively rather than adversarially.
A round hemisphere glued to a flat exterior disk is a highly regular rotationally symmetric model of a corner, used illustratively rather than adversarially.
A round hemisphere glued along a round circle to a Euclidean exterior is rotationally symmetric and is used only as the model picture for a corner.
a round hemisphere and round exterior disk glued along a round circle form a highly rotationally symmetric model corner, used only illustratively
A rotationally symmetric round/flat model used only to illustrate a manifold with a corner.
A round hemisphere glued to a Euclidean exterior is a highly regular rotationally symmetric model of a corner, used only illustratively.
A round hemisphere glued to a Euclidean exterior is a highly rotationally symmetric corner model used only to illustrate the notion of a corner.
A rotationally symmetric round/Euclidean glued model used only to illustrate what a corner is.
used only as a model of a corner, and the round cap glued along a round circle to a Euclidean exterior is highly rotationally symmetric
a round hemisphere glued to a flat exterior disk is a highly rotationally regular model of a corner, used illustratively rather than to refute a claim
rotationally symmetric constant-curvature pieces glued along a round circle, used only to illustrate a manifold with a corner
round hemisphere glued to a round Euclidean exterior is a rotationally symmetric model used only to illustrate a corner

examples.name: constant mass-aspect tensor
examples.note: Taking the asymptotic tensor h to be a constant multiple of the round metric shows how the hyperbolic mass inequality reduces to the familiar scalar statement m >= 0.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker specializes the mass-aspect tensor to a constant multiple of the identity and evaluates the integrals to see the mass inequality reduce to m ≥ 0

passes.reason: A constant mass-aspect tensor, especially a constant multiple of the round metric, is rotationally invariant and is used to illustrate the mass inequality reducing to m ≥ 0.
A constant mass-aspect tensor is the isotropic pure round-sphere case reducing the mass inequality to the scalar mass statement.
A constant scalar multiple of the round mass-aspect tensor is isotropic under rotations and is used to illustrate the mass inequality reducing to m ≥ 0.
a constant multiple of the round metric is an isotropic mass-aspect tensor with maximal spherical symmetry, used to illustrate the mass inequality
The case h = m g0 is isotropic under the sphere rotations and is used only to reduce the mass inequality to m ≥ 0.
A constant multiple of the round metric as mass-aspect tensor is SO(n)-invariant and used as a sanity-check special case of the mass inequality.
A constant multiple of the round metric as mass-aspect tensor is O(n)-invariant and is used as a simplifying illustrative case of the mass inequality.
An O(n)-invariant pure-trace mass-aspect tensor used to reduce the mass inequality to m≥0.
used to illustrate how the mass inequality reduces to m≥0, with a scalar-multiple-of-the-round-metric mass aspect that is rotationally invariant
a constant multiple of the round mass-aspect tensor is the isotropic case reducing the mass inequality to the usual scalar mass positivity
a constant multiple of the round metric is fully spherical-symmetry-invariant and is used to illustrate the mass inequality
an isotropic constant multiple of the round metric is rotationally symmetric and is used to recover the familiar scalar mass inequality

examples.name: compactly supported trace-free deformation of hyperbolic space
examples.note: The second-variation computation for a local trace-free perturbation of hyperbolic space visibly explains why one cannot locally raise scalar curvature above the hyperbolic value.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker computes first and second scalar-curvature variations for a compactly supported trace-free deformation and uses a weighted Poincare inequality to derive negativity

passes.reason: A compactly supported trace-free perturbation is a standard test deformation, not structurally sporadic, limiting, extremal, or maximally symmetric, and is used in a rigidity calculation rather than as a counterexample.
A compactly supported trace-free perturbation is structurally an ordinary local variation, but it is used to obstruct any local scalar-curvature-raising deformation of hyperbolic space.
A compactly supported trace-free perturbation is structurally ordinary within that constrained class and is used to show that local deformations cannot raise hyperbolic scalar curvature.
a compactly supported trace-free perturbation is a standard test deformation in the rigidity calculation, not a sporadic, extremal, or refuting example
A generic local trace-free perturbation is used to show that compactly supported deformations cannot raise hyperbolic scalar curvature.

An arbitrary compactly supported trace-free perturbation is structurally typical, but it is used as a would-be local scalar-curvature-raising deformation whose second variation obstructs that construction.
The compactly supported trace-free perturbation is used as a local scalar-curvature-raising attempt whose negative second variation obstructs such a construction.
Used as the attempted local perturbation whose variation calculation shows compactly supported scalar-curvature raising cannot work.
used as a local test deformation showing that attempts to raise hyperbolic scalar curvature by compactly supported perturbations cannot work
an arbitrary compactly supported trace-free variation is used as a test deformation for local rigidity, with no sporadic, extremal, symmetric, or collapsed structure
a generic compactly supported trace-free perturbation is used as a test obstruction to locally raising hyperbolic scalar curvature
a generic compactly supported trace-free perturbation is used as an attempted local scalar-curvature-raising deformation that the variation calculation obstructs

examples.name: spherical inner-product analogy
examples.note: The speaker compares the hyperbolic formula cosh d = Minkowski inner product with the elementary spherical formula relating distance to the Euclidean inner product, clarifying the asymptotic limit calculation.
examples.engagement_reason: the sphere inner-product fact is invoked only as an analogy for the hyperbolic distance formula, with no work carried out on the spherical case itself

passes.reason: The round sphere inner-product distance formula invokes the sphere as a maximally symmetric constant-curvature model for analogy, not to refute a claim.
The analogy uses the standard round sphere, a maximally symmetric space, simply to explain the inner-product distance formula.
The round-sphere inner-product distance formula comes from the maximally symmetric sphere and is used only as an explanatory analogy.

The round sphere inner-product formula is a maximally symmetric analogy for the hyperbolic distance formula.
The unit round sphere with its inner-product distance formula is a maximally symmetric comparison object used only as an analogy.
The standard sphere inner-product formula is invoked only as an analogy, and the round sphere is maximally symmetric.
